title: RESTful API Design
description: REST (Representational State Transfer) is the dominant architectural style for building web APIs. A well-designed REST API uses HTTP methods correctly, returns meaningful status codes, and structures URLs around resources rather than actions.
key_concepts: Use nouns for endpoint paths: /users, /products, not /getUser or /createProduct
HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove)
Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error
Stateless: each request must contain all information needed to process it
Versioning strategies: URL versioning (/v1/users), header versioning, query param versioning
Pagination: cursor-based pagination scales better than offset for large datasets
HATEOAS (Hypermedia) links resources together for discoverability
tips: Always validate and sanitize request input server-side
Use HTTPS in production — never expose APIs over plain HTTP
Return consistent error shapes: {error, message, code}
Rate-limit public endpoints to prevent abuse

title: SQL Databases and Query Optimization
description: Relational databases organize data into tables with defined schemas and support ACID transactions. Knowing how to write efficient SQL and design schemas correctly is foundational for backend engineers.
key_concepts: ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — guarantees that make transactions reliable
Indexing: B-tree indexes speed up lookups but slow down writes; index columns used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY
Query execution plan: use EXPLAIN to understand how the database executes a query
Normalization: organize data to reduce redundancy (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)
Denormalization: introduce redundancy deliberately to avoid expensive joins for read-heavy workloads
Connection pooling: reuse database connections to reduce overhead (PgBouncer, HikariCP)
N+1 query problem: loading N related records with N separate queries instead of one JOIN
Transactions and isolation levels: READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE
tips: Prefer JOINs over separate queries for related data
Avoid SELECT * in production — specify only needed columns
Use prepared statements to prevent SQL injection
Monitor slow query logs and set query timeouts

title: Caching Strategies
description: Caching stores precomputed results to serve future requests faster. Effective caching reduces database load, decreases latency, and improves throughput. Choosing the right caching strategy is critical for scalable systems.
key_concepts: Cache-aside (lazy loading): application checks cache first; on miss, fetches from DB and populates cache
Write-through: write to cache and DB simultaneously — keeps cache consistent but adds write latency
Write-behind (write-back): write to cache first, persist to DB asynchronously — faster writes, risk of data loss
TTL (Time To Live): cache entries expire after a set duration to prevent stale data
Cache eviction policies: LRU (Least Recently Used), LFU (Least Frequently Used), FIFO
Cache invalidation: explicitly remove or update cache entries when source data changes
Distributed cache: Redis or Memcached shared across multiple application instances
CDN caching: edge nodes cache static assets and responses close to users
tips: Cache at the layer closest to the user when possible
Never cache sensitive user-specific data without scoping by user ID
Monitor cache hit rates — below 80% usually indicates poor cache key design
Plan cache warm-up strategy for cold starts after deployments

title: Authentication and Authorization
description: Authentication verifies who a user is; authorization decides what they can do. Getting security right in these two areas is critical — mistakes lead to data breaches and privilege escalation.
key_concepts: JWT (JSON Web Token): stateless token containing claims; signed with a secret or RSA key pair
OAuth 2.0: delegation framework — allows third parties to act on behalf of a user without sharing credentials
OpenID Connect (OIDC): identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 for authentication
Session-based auth: server stores session state; client holds a session cookie
RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): permissions assigned to roles, roles assigned to users
ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): fine-grained policies based on user, resource, and environment attributes
MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication): adds a second factor (TOTP, SMS, hardware key) beyond password
Password hashing: use bcrypt, Argon2, or scrypt — never store plaintext or MD5/SHA1 hashed passwords
HTTPS and secure cookies: use Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite flags on session cookies
tips: Keep JWT expiry short (15–60 min) and use refresh tokens
Always validate and sanitize inputs before checking permissions
Log all authentication failures for security monitoring
Rotate secrets regularly and on suspected compromise

title: Message Queues and Asynchronous Processing
description: Message queues decouple producers from consumers, allowing systems to handle spikes in load and process work asynchronously. This is essential for reliable, scalable backend systems.
key_concepts: Producer-consumer pattern: one service publishes messages, another consumes and processes them
At-least-once delivery: message may be delivered more than once; consumers must be idempotent
Exactly-once delivery: guaranteed but expensive; usually avoided by designing idempotent consumers
Dead-letter queue (DLQ): messages that fail processing after N retries land here for inspection
Pub/Sub vs point-to-point: pub/sub broadcasts to multiple subscribers; point-to-point targets one consumer
Backpressure: consumer signals it is overwhelmed so producer slows down
Event sourcing: store all state changes as an immutable event log instead of current state
tips: Design all consumers to be idempotent from day one
Monitor queue depth and consumer lag as key SLIs
Set appropriate message retention and DLQ policies
Use Kafka for high-throughput event streaming; SQS for simple task queues

title: Microservices Architecture
description: Microservices decompose a monolith into small, independently deployable services that communicate over a network. The pattern improves scalability and team autonomy but adds operational complexity.
key_concepts: Service boundary design: start with domain-driven design (DDD) bounded contexts
Synchronous communication: REST or gRPC for request-response interactions between services
Asynchronous communication: events via message queues for loose coupling and resilience
Service discovery: consul, Kubernetes DNS, or AWS Cloud Map let services find each other dynamically
API Gateway: single entry point that routes, authenticates, and rate-limits inter-service traffic
Circuit breaker: prevent cascading failures by failing fast when a downstream service is unhealthy
Saga pattern: manage distributed transactions across services using a sequence of local transactions
Distributed tracing: correlation IDs and tools like Jaeger/X-Ray to trace requests across services
tips: Do not prematurely decompose — start with a modular monolith
Each service should own its own database to avoid tight coupling
Prefer async communication to reduce temporal coupling
Invest in observability (logs, metrics, traces) before going to prod

title: Supervised Learning Fundamentals
description: Supervised learning trains a model on labeled data — input-output pairs — so it can predict outputs for unseen inputs. It is the most widely applied ML paradigm in industry.
key_concepts: Training set, validation set, test set: never evaluate on training data — it leads to overfitting
Bias-variance tradeoff: high bias = underfitting (too simple), high variance = overfitting (too complex)
Loss function: quantifies prediction error; MSE for regression, cross-entropy for classification
Gradient descent: iteratively adjusts weights in the direction that minimizes the loss
Learning rate: step size during gradient descent; too high diverges, too low is slow
Regularization: L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) penalize large weights to reduce overfitting
Cross-validation: k-fold CV gives reliable performance estimate when data is limited
Feature engineering: creating informative input features often has more impact than algorithm choice
tips: Establish a simple baseline (mean prediction, logistic regression) before trying complex models
More data usually beats a better algorithm
Check class imbalance — use stratified splits and appropriate metrics (F1, AUC-ROC)
Always look at your data before modeling — EDA catches issues early

title: Deep Learning and Neural Networks
description: Deep learning uses multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations from data. It powers most modern AI breakthroughs in vision, NLP, and speech.
key_concepts: Neuron: applies a weighted sum of inputs then a non-linear activation function (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh)
Backpropagation: computes gradients of the loss with respect to every weight via the chain rule
Batch normalization: normalizes layer inputs to stabilize and speed training
Dropout: randomly zeroes activations during training to prevent co-adaptation and overfitting
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): use shared-weight filters to learn spatial features; dominant in computer vision
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) / LSTMs: maintain hidden state for sequential data; mostly replaced by Transformers
Transformer architecture: self-attention mechanism; basis for BERT, GPT, and all modern LLMs
Transfer learning: fine-tune a pretrained model on your task instead of training from scratch
Mixed precision training: use float16 to halve memory and speed up GPU training
tips: Use pretrained models (ImageNet, BERT) as starting points — don't train from scratch unless you have massive data
Monitor training and validation loss curves — divergence signals overfitting
Use a learning rate scheduler (cosine annealing, reduce on plateau)
Profile GPU utilization — most bottlenecks are data loading, not compute

title: MLOps and Model Deployment
description: MLOps applies DevOps principles to machine learning to automate training, evaluation, and deployment of models. A model that can't reach production creates no value.
key_concepts: Experiment tracking: log hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts for every run (MLflow, W&B)
Model registry: versioned store of trained models with metadata and lifecycle states
Feature store: centralized repository of computed features to avoid training-serving skew
Continuous training (CT): automatically retrain when data distribution shifts or scheduled
Model monitoring: track prediction drift, data drift, and business KPIs in production
Shadow deployment: run new model in parallel, log predictions, compare offline before switching
A/B testing: route a traffic split to two model versions and measure which performs better
Containerization: package model + dependencies into a Docker image for reproducible serving
tips: Treat ML pipelines as code — version control everything including preprocessing
Test models like software: unit tests for features, integration tests for predictions
Log enough metadata to reproduce any training run
Automate retraining triggers based on data drift thresholds

title: Natural Language Processing (NLP)
description: NLP enables computers to understand, generate, and manipulate human language. Modern NLP is dominated by large pre-trained Transformer models fine-tuned for specific tasks.
key_concepts: Tokenization: splitting text into subword tokens (BPE, WordPiece) used as model input
Word embeddings: dense vector representations; Word2Vec showed words with similar contexts are close in vector space
Attention mechanism: allows the model to weigh which input tokens are relevant for each output token
BERT: bidirectional encoder; good for classification, NER, QA
GPT: autoregressive decoder; good for generation tasks
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): augment LLM context with retrieved documents to reduce hallucination
Prompt engineering: crafting input prompts to elicit desired behavior from LLMs without fine-tuning
Fine-tuning: updating a pretrained model on a domain-specific dataset; LoRA/QLoRA for parameter-efficient fine-tuning
tips: Always evaluate LLM outputs — they hallucinate confidently
Use RAG to ground LLM answers in your own verified documents
For classification tasks, fine-tuning a small BERT model often beats prompting a large LLM
Clean and deduplicate your text data before training — garbage in, garbage out

title: Docker and Containerization
description: Docker packages an application and its dependencies into a portable container that runs consistently across environments. Containers are the foundation of modern cloud deployment.
key_concepts: Image: immutable blueprint built from a Dockerfile; contains OS, runtime, dependencies, and app code
Container: a running instance of an image — isolated process with its own filesystem, network, and PID namespace
Dockerfile: step-by-step instructions to build an image (FROM, RUN, COPY, ENV, EXPOSE, CMD)
Multi-stage builds: use multiple FROM statements to keep the final image small by discarding build tools
Volumes: persist data outside the container lifecycle; bind mounts for development, named volumes for production
Networking: containers on the same bridge network can reach each other by service name
Docker Compose: define multi-container apps in a YAML file; single command to start the whole stack
Layer caching: each Dockerfile instruction creates a cache layer — order instructions from least to most frequent change
tips: Use official slim/alpine base images to minimize attack surface and image size
Never run containers as root — use USER instruction in Dockerfile
Copy requirements.txt and install dependencies before copying app code to maximize cache hits
Use .dockerignore to exclude node_modules, .venv, .git from the build context

title: Kubernetes Orchestration
description: Kubernetes (K8s) automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It is the de-facto standard for running containers in production at scale.
key_concepts: Pod: smallest deployable unit; one or more tightly coupled containers sharing network and storage
Deployment: manages a ReplicaSet to ensure the desired number of Pod replicas are running
Service: stable DNS name and virtual IP for a set of Pods; types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer
Ingress: HTTP/S routing rules that expose services externally via a reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik)
ConfigMap / Secret: decouple configuration and sensitive data from container images
HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA): automatically scale Deployment replicas based on CPU/memory or custom metrics
Persistent Volume (PV) / Persistent Volume Claim (PVC): provision and consume storage independently of Pods
Namespace: virtual cluster within K8s for isolating resources across teams or environments
Health probes: livenessProbe restarts unhealthy containers; readinessProbe removes them from load balancer
tips: Always set resource requests and limits on containers to enable correct scheduling
Use Helm charts to version and template Kubernetes manifests
Never store secrets in ConfigMaps or plain manifests — use Sealed Secrets or Vault
Run readiness probes on all HTTP services to avoid routing traffic to booting Pods

title: AWS Core Services for Backend Engineers
description: AWS is the leading cloud provider. Backend engineers use a core set of services for compute, storage, databases, queues, and security. Understanding these is essential for building cloud-native applications.
key_concepts: EC2: virtual machines; choose instance type based on compute (c), memory (r), or GPU (p/g) requirements
Lambda: serverless functions that scale to zero and charge per invocation — ideal for event-driven workloads
ECS / EKS: container orchestration services built on top of EC2 for running Docker containers at scale
S3: object storage for files, backups, static assets; 11 nines durability
RDS / Aurora: managed relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL); Aurora Serverless scales automatically
DynamoDB: serverless NoSQL key-value and document store; single-digit millisecond latency at any scale
SQS / SNS: managed queue and pub/sub messaging; foundational for decoupled microservices
IAM: identity and access management; least-privilege roles, policies, and instance profiles
VPC: isolated virtual network; public subnets for load balancers, private subnets for compute and databases
CloudWatch: logs, metrics, alarms, and dashboards; central observability platform on AWS
tips: Use IAM roles — never hard-code access keys in application code or containers
Enable VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail for security auditing
Tag all resources with environment, team, and cost-center for cost allocation
Use S3 lifecycle policies to automatically move old data to Glacier (cheaper storage tier)

title: Serverless Architecture
description: Serverless lets you run code without managing servers. You pay only for execution time and the cloud auto-scales to zero. It reduces operational overhead but requires different design patterns.
key_concepts: Function as a Service (FaaS): Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions
Cold start: first invocation boots a new execution environment, adding latency — mitigate with provisioned concurrency
Event-driven triggers: functions respond to S3 uploads, SQS messages, API Gateway requests, DynamoDB streams
Stateless functions: do not rely on local state between invocations — use DynamoDB, S3, or Redis for state
Dead-letter queues (DLQ): capture failed Lambda invocations for analysis and retry
Concurrency limits: Lambda has a regional concurrency limit — burst traffic can throttle functions
Step Functions: orchestrate complex multi-step workflows using state machines without polling loops
API Gateway: fully managed HTTP endpoint in front of Lambda; supports REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs
tips: Keep Lambda packages small — strip dev dependencies to reduce cold start time
Use environment variables for configuration — never bake secrets into the deployment package
Set reserved concurrency to protect downstream services from being overwhelmed
Write idempotent Lambda handlers — SQS delivers at least once

title: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
description: Infrastructure as Code manages cloud resources through machine-readable configuration files rather than manual console clicks. It enables version control, consistency, and automated provisioning.
key_concepts: Declarative vs imperative: Terraform and CDK let you declare desired state; the tool figures out the plan
Terraform: HCL-based IaC; provider ecosystem covers AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, and more
AWS CDK: define infrastructure using TypeScript, Python, or Java; synthesizes to CloudFormation
State file: Terraform tracks actual resource state in a .tfstate file — store remotely in S3 + DynamoDB for teams
Plan before apply: always run terraform plan to review changes before terraform apply
Modules: reusable infrastructure components that accept inputs and produce outputs
Drift detection: detect when actual cloud state diverges from declared state
GitOps: store IaC in git; use PRs for review and CI/CD pipelines to apply changes automatically
tips: Keep state files in a remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB lock) — never commit state to git
Use separate workspaces or directories for dev/staging/prod environments
Tag everything at the module level to ensure consistent resource tagging
Run terraform validate and tflint in CI to catch errors before review

title: CI/CD Pipelines
description: Continuous Integration automates building and testing code on every commit. Continuous Delivery/Deployment automates releasing to staging or production. Fast, reliable CI/CD is a force multiplier for engineering teams.
key_concepts: Continuous Integration: every commit triggers a build and automated test suite
Continuous Delivery: every green build is a release candidate deployable to production with one click
Continuous Deployment: every green build is automatically deployed to production without human approval
Build stages: lint → unit test → integration test → security scan → build artifact → deploy
Artifact registry: Docker images or compiled binaries stored by immutable tags (git SHA, semver)
Blue/green deployment: two identical environments; switch traffic from blue (old) to green (new) instantly
Canary release: route a small percentage of traffic to new version; full rollout after validation
Rollback strategy: keep previous artifact; re-deploy or switch traffic back on failure
tips: Keep build times under 10 minutes — developers stop watching slow pipelines
Fail fast: run the cheapest checks (lint) first, expensive checks (integration tests) last
Pin action versions to a SHA for security — avoid using @latest
Store secrets in the CI platform's secret store — never in YAML files

title: Software Testing Best Practices
description: Testing validates that software behaves as expected and catches regressions before they reach users. A good testing strategy balances speed, confidence, and maintenance cost.
key_concepts: Test pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests, fewest E2E tests (slow, expensive)
Unit test: tests one function or class in isolation; dependencies are mocked
Integration test: tests how components interact — e.g., service + database, or two microservices
End-to-end (E2E) test: simulates a real user flow through the full system
Test coverage: aim for high coverage on business logic; 100% coverage of trivial code is waste
Test doubles: mocks (verify calls), stubs (return values), fakes (lightweight implementations)
Test-Driven Development (TDD): write the test first, then the minimal code to pass it
Property-based testing: generate random inputs to find edge cases automatically (Hypothesis for Python)
tips: Treat test code with the same care as production code — refactor, review, and document it
Tests that take more than 1 second to run belong in the integration layer, not unit layer
Use parameterized tests to cover multiple input scenarios without duplicating code
Mock external services (AWS, Stripe) at the boundary — not deep in business logic

title: Code Review Best Practices
description: Code reviews improve quality, share knowledge, and catch bugs before they reach production. Effective reviews are timely, constructive, and focused on the code rather than the author.
key_concepts: PR size: smaller PRs (< 400 lines) are reviewed more thoroughly and merged faster
Reviewer checklist: correctness, readability, test coverage, security, performance, backward compatibility
Constructive feedback: suggest improvements with reasoning; ask questions instead of issuing commands
Author responsibility: write a clear PR description, link the issue/ticket, and self-review before requesting review
Automate what can be automated: lint, format, and type-check in CI — leave human reviewers for logic and design
Blocking vs non-blocking comments: distinguish between must-fix and nice-to-have feedback
Pair programming as an alternative: some teams use pairing instead of async review for high-risk changes
tips: Review within 24 hours — stale PRs kill team velocity
Acknowledge what the author did well — not just what needs fixing
Do not nitpick style that a formatter can fix automatically
If a review takes more than 90 minutes, the PR is probably too large

title: System Design Fundamentals
description: System design interviews and real-world architecture decisions require reasoning about scalability, reliability, and trade-offs. Core principles apply whether you are designing a URL shortener or a global payment platform.
key_concepts: Scalability: vertical scaling adds resources to one machine; horizontal scaling adds more machines
Load balancing: distributes traffic across servers; strategies include round-robin, least connections, and IP hash
CAP theorem: distributed systems can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
PACELC: extends CAP to account for latency trade-offs even when the network is healthy
Sharding: partition a database horizontally so each shard holds a subset of rows; enables horizontal scaling
Replication: copy data to multiple nodes for redundancy; leader-follower vs multi-leader vs leaderless
Consistent hashing: distribute data across a changing set of nodes with minimal redistribution on scale changes
Idempotency: designing operations so they can safely be retried without side effects
tips: Start with requirements: functional (what it does) and non-functional (scale, latency, SLA)
Estimate scale early: QPS, storage, bandwidth — back-of-envelope math anchors design decisions
Identify the bottleneck before optimizing; premature optimization wastes engineering time
Every design involves trade-offs — always explain why you chose one approach over another

title: Observability: Logging, Metrics, and Tracing
description: Observability allows engineers to understand the internal state of a system purely from its external outputs. The three pillars — logs, metrics, and traces — together provide the full picture needed to debug production incidents.
key_concepts: Structured logging: emit JSON logs with consistent fields (timestamp, level, service, trace_id, message) for machine parsing
Log levels: DEBUG (verbose), INFO (normal), WARNING (degraded), ERROR (failure), CRITICAL (system down)
Metrics: numeric time-series data; counters (requests_total), gauges (memory_used), histograms (request_duration)
RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration — three service-level metrics that tell you if users are being affected
USE method: Utilization, Saturation, Errors — for infrastructure (CPU, disk, network)
Distributed tracing: a trace spans multiple services; spans are the individual units of work within a trace
Correlation ID: a unique ID generated at the entry point and propagated through all downstream calls
SLI / SLO / SLA: Service Level Indicators measured against Objectives agreed to in a Service Level Agreement
tips: Log at the boundary of your service — every inbound request and outbound call
Do not log PII — mask or omit sensitive fields before writing to logs
Alert on symptoms (high error rate, elevated latency) not causes (high CPU) — causes are for dashboards
Instrument before you need it — adding observability during an incident is impossible

title: Git and Version Control Workflows
description: Git is the standard version control system. Understanding branching strategies, rebasing, and collaborative workflows is essential for working effectively in any engineering team.
key_concepts: Git branching models: GitHub Flow (main + feature branches), Git Flow (main, develop, release, hotfix)
Rebase vs merge: rebase rewrites history for a linear log; merge preserves history with a merge commit
Interactive rebase: squash fixup commits, reorder, edit messages before opening a PR
Cherry-pick: apply a specific commit from one branch to another — useful for hotfixes
Tags and semantic versioning: v1.2.3 — major.minor.patch; use annotated tags for releases
Stash: temporarily shelve uncommitted changes with git stash; restore with git stash pop
Bisect: binary search through commits to find which commit introduced a bug
Hooks: pre-commit hooks run linting and tests locally before committing
tips: Write commit messages in imperative mood: 'Add feature' not 'Added feature'
Never force-push to shared branches like main or develop
Use .gitignore from the start — committing secrets or build artifacts is painful to undo
Regularly fetch and rebase on main to reduce merge conflicts
